sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Software engineering applies disciplined processes and technical methods so that software both satisfies reliability/quality requirements and can be produced and maintained within realistic cost constraints. The two goals must be balanced, so reliable and cost-effective software is the best answer.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Reliability without affordability may make a project infeasible, while cheap but unreliable software fails its purpose. Each single-goal option is incomplete.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Engineering optimizes required quality under economic and schedule constraints.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Top-down design decomposes a system from high-level modules into progressively more detailed submodules. It relies on stepwise refinement and modularity, and may use flowcharts to express control. Loop invariants are a program-verification technique for proving loop correctness, not a requirement of top-down design.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Stepwise refinement is the essence of top-down decomposition; modularity is its structural result; flowcharting is a compatible design notation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Do not confuse a design/decomposition method with a formal loop-proof technique.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The classic waterfall model is the simplest conceptual lifecycle model: requirements, design, implementation, testing, deployment, and maintenance proceed as a mostly linear sequence of phases.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Prototyping and iterative models add feedback cycles, repeated refinement, and version management, making their process structure more complex.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Waterfall is the basic linear phase-by-phase development model.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Software design usually begins with the overall architecture and successively decomposes it into subsystems, modules, interfaces, and detailed operations. This is a top-down progression.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Bottom-up design can be used when composing reusable components but is not the usual answer to the general lifecycle question. Random and 'centre fringing' are not disciplined design strategies.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Move from system architecture to increasingly detailed component design.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: Application software is created to perform a user-oriented task, such as word processing, accounting, browsing, or image editing. Option C gives this defining property.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Controlling the operating system is a system-software role. Compilers, debuggers, and similar programmer aids are programming/system tools, so A and B do not define application software and D cannot be true.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Application software serves an end-user task; system software operates/supports the computer platform.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: For an interconnection/switching network, hardware cost is commonly modeled by switching complexity—the number and complexity of switching elements and interconnections required to realize its connectivity.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Time complexity measures algorithmic running time. 'Circuit complexity' is broader and less specific than the standard switching-network cost measure used in this context.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Network topology cost grows with the switching fabric needed to implement it.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: A T1 digital carrier provides a nominal 1.544 Mbit/s leased line in the North American hierarchy. It consists of 24 DS0 channels plus framing overhead.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: T2, T3, and T4 are successively higher-rate aggregate levels; T3, for example, is about 44.736 Mbit/s.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Remember the base high-speed leased digital carrier: T1 = 1.544 Mbit/s.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: The intended IS-95 fact is that one 1.25-MHz CDMA forward carrier is channelized by 64 orthogonal Walsh codes, yielding 64 logical radio/code channels (including pilot, sync, paging, and traffic roles). Thus 64 is the intended count. The stem imprecisely calls these 'carriers'; technically it is one 1.25-MHz RF carrier containing up to 64 code channels.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: 9, 18, and 22 are not the Walsh-code channelization count associated with an IS-95 1.25-MHz carrier.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: CDMA shares one wide RF carrier by orthogonal codes rather than assigning one separate carrier per user.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: PVM may manage multiple message buffers, but at any given instant a task designates exactly one active send buffer and one active receive buffer. Packing/sending operates on the active send buffer; receiving/unpacking operates on the active receive buffer.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: Options B–D claim two simultaneously active buffers on one side, contrary to PVM's active-buffer model.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Multiple buffers can exist, but PVM exposes one active buffer in each direction at a time.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.explanation: For a predictive model, the data set supplies training examples, the output set supplies the target values/classes the model must predict, and a separate test set measures generalization on unseen examples. Of the offered combinations, i,v,vi is the coherent supervised-learning workflow.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.whyOthersFail: An 'information set' and 'process set' are not the standard three datasets required to train and evaluate a predictive model. Options A, B, and D omit either the actual data or the held-out test set. The source terminology is loose; modern practice would more precisely say training inputs, target outputs, and validation/test data.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2010-june-paper-2.pdf.keyIdea: Predictive learning needs examples, targets, and unseen evaluation data.
